english: complaints, lamentations Dzl.; skad čéṅ-po (skad chen po) to cry aloud Dzl.; to exhibit, to extol, bstán-pai čé-ba (bstan pa'i che ba) the grandeur of the doctrine Tar. 48, 9, Schf.; to drive out, turn out, expel, gnas °byin-pa (gnas 'byin pa) Tar., *piṅ-wa* (ping wa) Ts., to banish, so also Ld. *piṅ-čè* (ping che); to cast out, throw away Ts.; to save, rescue, liberate, release, nas (nas) from, Dom.; absol. Tar. 121, 19. — 3. particular phrases, such as kól-du pyúṅ-ba ('khol du phyung ba), yid °byin-pa (yid 'byin pa) etc. v. in their own places.

pf. and imp. byugs (byugs) 1. to wet, moisten, smear, spread over, anoint, with la (la): ša skám-la tsá-ču byúgs-pa (sha skam la tsha chu byugs pa) salt-meat Glr.; gdóṅ-la sol-snúm °byúg-pa (gdong la sol snum 'byug pa) to daub one's face with coal-salve Glr.; also with accus. and instrum.: lha-rtén spos daṅ byúg-pas (lha rten spos dang byug pas) covering the little temple with spices and ointments Dzl.; gser °byúg-pa (gser 'byug pa) prob. to gild Pth. — 2. to stroke, to pat, mgó la (mgo la) a person's head Dzl.

1. vb., pf. imp. byuṅ (byung) (intrs of °byin-pa ('byin pa)) to come out, to emerge, often with a pleon. pyir (phyir) etc., from the water, from an egg, a vessel etc. Dzl.; °kór-ba-las ('khor ba las) — to be set free, to be liberated Dom.; to go out, kyím-nas (khyim nas) Dzl.; pyi-rol-tu °byuṅ-ba (phyi rol tu 'byung ba) to go out into the open air Dzl.; to make one's appearance, to become visible Dzl.; to show one's self, to appear rgyál-poi rmi-lam-du byuṅ-bai lha-yčig (rgyal po'i rmi lam du byung ba'i lha gcig) the princess that appeared to the king in a dream Glr.; also: ṅá-la rmi-lam bzáṅ-po byuṅ (nga la rmi lam bzang po byung) I have had an auspicious dream Mil.; sgréṅ-mor °byuṅ-ba (sgreng mor 'byung ba) to go abroad naked Dzl.; to be heard, to resound, skad (skad) frq.; to be said, to be told Tar.; to turn out, to prove, to be found, ma bzí-ba su byuṅ-ba (ma bzi ba su byung ba) he who is found not intoxicated Glr.; ṅán-pa byuṅ (ngan pa byung) it proved to be ill founded Mil.; ...pa su yaṅ ma byuṅ (...pa su yang ma byung) none was to be found that... Pth.; to step forward, from the crowd; to step forth, to appear Glr.; to step up to, with rtsar (rtsar) to Glr.; brgyúgs-nas byuṅ (brgyugs nas byung) they came running up or near Pth.; to go to, to proceed to, to come, rii rtsé-mor (ri'i rtse mor) Dzl.; *ka-náṅ-wa ma juṅ-na* (ka nang wa ma jung na) W. if no order (permission etc.) comes; dbugs pyir byuṅ-nas (dbugs phyir byung nas) when breathing returned, when they recovered from fainting Dzl.; mun-pai bskal-pa lṅa-brgya byuṅ-ṅo (mun pa'i bskal pa lnga brgya byung ngo) then came, followed, 500 dark Kalpas Pth. — 2. to rise, as kings, frq.; to arise, to originate, to become, with nas (nas), las (las), from, in consequence of, by, dé-nas byuṅ (de nas byung) it derives its origin from that Glr.; °brás-bu °byuṅ-bai šiṅ ('bras bu 'byung ba'i shing) trees on which fruit is growing Stg.; mi °byuṅ-bar gyúr ba (mi 'byung bar gyur ba) not to come to a fair beginning, to be suppressed in its first beginnings Glr.; kyeu žig byuṅ dug (khye'u zhig byung dug) by that time a boy had become of it Glr.; ynyis-su byuṅ (gnyis su byung) they became two, they split in two (systems of doctrine); ráb-tu °byuṅ-ba (rab tu 'byung ba) to become a priest, v. rab (rab); to come in (money); to happen, to take place, very frq., ltas čí byuṅ (ltas ci byung) what signs have taken place? Dzl.; mi žíg-la nyés-pa čéṅ-po byuṅ (mi zhig la nyes pa chen po byung) = a man has committed etc. Dzl. frq.; ro °di-rnams-la čí byuṅ-ba yin (ro 'di rnams la ci byung ba yin) what has happened to these corpses, what is their history? Glr.; sṅar byuṅ-ba (sngar byung ba) and ma byuṅ-ba (ma byung ba) things heard of and unheard of Tar.; kà-pye-nas yód-pa dé-aṅ de dús-su byuṅ-ṅo (kha phye nas yod pa de ang de dus su byung ngo) 'at that time also the opened position (of the hands of the image) took place' Glr.; blá-ma-la yaṅ byuṅ lágs-sam (bla ma la yang byung lags sam) did the same thing happen to your Reverence? Mil.; ṅéd-kyis °dí-bžin byuṅ (nged kyis 'di bzhin byung) it is I that brought this thing about Glr.; pyis-byuṅ (phyis byung) or °byuṅ ('byung) the later time, time to come, also adv. afterwards, latterly, Tar. — 3. The word more and more assumes the character of an auxiliary in such phrases as the following: °gro-tub-pa byuṅ ('gro thub pa byung) they were able to proceed (the possibility of proceeding was brought about) Glr.; da bla-ma der bžúgs-pa byuṅ-na (da bla ma der bzhugs pa byung na) in case your Reverence should stay there Mil.; with the supine: °búl-du ('bul du), žér-du (zher du), stón-du byuṅ-ba-la(s) (ston du byung ba la(s)) as they gave, said, showed Mil.; tugs-dám °pél-bar byuṅ (thugs dam 'phel bar byung) meditation increased; lastly, with the root only: bod daṅ °brel byuṅ (bod dang 'brel byung) came into communication with Tibet Glr.; sleb byuṅ-ba-la (sleb byung ba la) when he appeared Mil.; rdo dbyug byuṅ (rdo dbyug byung) he threw a stone; and so it is commonly used now, esp. in C.; it supplies the place of a copula in: ysuṅ de kun séms-la šin-tu °tád-pa žig byuṅ (gsung de kun sems la shin tu 'thad pa zhig byung) this song was truly heart-affecting Mil.
